text: We present a case of laparoscopic total gastrectomy, omentectomy, splenectomy, or gastric adenocarcinoma. A seventy-six year old male presents with past medical history of pernicious anemia, hypertension, transient ischemic attack, hypothyroidism, dyslipidemia, pulmonary embolism, and prostate cancer. His past surgical history included open suprapubic subtotal prostatectomy done eight years ago and a bilateral inguinal hernia repair, both done at an outside hospital. He underwent screening upper gastrointestinal endoscopy for anemia at our institution, which showed a small size lesion in the fundus of stomach. Biopsies obtained from the lesion came diagnostic for gastric adenocarcinoma. Preoperatively, placement of IVC filter was done. This image confirms a small size mass in the fundus of the stomach.

The surgeon performs diagnostic laparoscopy, beginning with the dissection of the greater omentum from the transverse colon, moving from the left to the right side. Dissection continues along the gastroepiploic vessels and station IV lymph nodes. The retroduodenal space is dissected, skeletonizing station V and VI lymph nodes near the suprapyloric and intrapyloric areas. The duodenum is transected distal to the pylorus, and the duodenal stump is oversewn with two OO absorbable sutures. Dissection proceeds along the pars flaccida and station III lymph nodes on the lesser curvature, where the left gastric vessels are identified, dissected from station VII lymph nodes, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon dissects the retrogastric space, skeletonizing station eleven lymph nodes. Dissection continues along the right and left crura, skeletonizing station one and two lymph nodes. The phrenoesophageal fat pad is dissected, and the esophagus is mobilized into the abdominal cavity before being transected. Dissection progresses along the greater curvature of the stomach towards the spleen, detaching posterior and lateral attachments. The splenic hilum is identified, and splenectomy is completed with specimen retrieval. A Roux-en-Y operation is performed, including esophago-jejunostomy with a 25 mm circular stapler, followed by jejuno-jejunostomy between the biliopancreatic limb and common channel. Anchoring sutures are placed on the crura for a tension-free anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a leak test to assess the patency of the esophagojejunostomy anastomosis, ensuring there are no leaks at the surgical site.

The specimens collected were total gastrectomy, omentectomy and splenectomy and there were no intraoperative complications. The surgical pathology of the specimens retrieved showed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with dysplasia in background of chronic atrophic gastritis with multifocal intestinal metaplasia. Metastatic adenocarcinoma was seen in four of the twenty-eight lymph nodes retrieved. Omentum showed no evidence of tumor. Spleen was histologically undemarkable and the splenic artery showed focal arterial calcification at splenic hilum. Two splenic hilar lymph nodes came negative for cancer. This was T-II, N-I, M-Zero cancer and stage II according to AJCC classification.

The patient was transitioned to a fluid diet by post-operative day II. He received an upper GI series which came back normal on post-operative day III. The drains were removed and the drain amylase came negative on the post-operative day five. The patient was discharged home in a stable condition on a post-operative day six with adequate pain control and the bowel movements present.
He was started on a Folfox therapy for twelve cycles on post-operative week seven. Here's an image showing the normal upper GI series. on the post-operative day three. Thank you.
